title: Cat Licked Antifreeze: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat licked antifreeze, it is a high-urgency emergency. Act now to prevent kidney failure. Learn the immediate steps to take.
content_html:
TL;DR: Antifreeze ingestion is a life-threatening emergency for cats that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent fatal kidney failure. Contact an emergency vet or pet poison control immediately if you suspect any exposure, even if your cat seems fine.

What is antifreeze poisoning and why is it dangerous for my cat?

Antifreeze commonly contains ethylene glycol, a chemical that is extremely toxic to cats. Because it has a sweet taste, cats may be tempted to lick even small spills or drops. Once ingested, the body breaks it down into toxic metabolites that rapidly cause severe, irreversible damage to the kidneys.

Is antifreeze ingestion a medical emergency for my cat?

  • Yes, this is a High-Urgency Emergency. There is no safe amount of antifreeze for a cat to consume.
  • Even a few drops licked off a paw or the floor can be fatal.
  • If you suspect your cat has had any contact with antifreeze, you must contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear, as internal damage begins almost instantly.

How does taking a photo of the antifreeze container help my veterinarian?

  • If it is safe to do so, take a clear photo of the product container or the spill.
  • Different brands use different chemical concentrations; for example, some contain propylene glycol, which is slightly less toxic than ethylene glycol but still dangerous.
  • Showing the label to your veterinarian helps them identify the exact toxin and determine the most effective treatment or antidote immediately upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of ethylene glycol (EG) by a cat, rapid absorption from the gastrointestinal tract occurs, with peak blood concentrations reached within 3 hours. The minimum lethal dose of undiluted EG is 1.4 mL/kg body weight in cats. Diagnosis can be aided by detecting EG concentrations in serum and urine as early as 1-2 hours post-ingestion, with commercial test kits able to detect serum EG concentrations of ≥50 mg/dL. Some antifreeze preparations contain fluorescein, which fluoresces bright yellow-green under a Wood's lamp, and urine fluorescence can be a qualitative adjunctive test. Activated charcoal is not likely to reduce absorption of EG. Once absorbed, treatment focuses on increasing EG excretion through fluid therapy to correct dehydration and promote urine production. In dogs, 4-Methylpyrazole (4-MP, fomepizole) is the treatment of choice to prevent EG metabolism by inactivating alcohol dehydrogenase. However, in cats, 4-MP is ineffective at the canine dosage.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3048)

faqs.question: What are the early symptoms of antifreeze poisoning in cats?
faqs.answer: Within 30 minutes to 12 hours, cats may appear 'drunk,' showing signs like unsteadiness, vomiting, excessive thirst, and lethargy.

faqs.question: How long do I have to get my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Minutes matter. Treatment is most effective if started within 3 hours of ingestion. Waiting longer than 8 to 12 hours significantly decreases the chances of survival.

faqs.question: Can I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to induce vomiting in a cat at home unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian, as this can lead to further complications like aspiration pneumonia.
